coredata.dc:title: Water friendly room temperature phosphorescence doped materials prepared via metal organic framework matrix transformation
coredata.dc:creator: Ni, Ai-Yun
Zhang, Bo-Lun
Zhang, Pei-Pei
Zhang, Jian-Jun
Wang, Hui-Yu
Feng, Ke-Xin
Liu, Shuqin
Ni, Jun
Duan, Chunying
coredata.dc:description: Stimuli-responsive room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have wide applications. However, it is still a challenge to achieve water-induced recovery afterglow materials, and materials can maintain afterglow after prolonged contact with water. Herein we show that a RTP-silent metal-organic framework (MOF), MOF-5, can immediately emit green RTP upon water stimulation. Detailed studies show water causes transformation of MOF-5 into another more tightly packed framework. Besides, strong hydrogen bond network is constructed by H2O molecules trapped in the channels. Both factors limit the motion of the ligand and stabilize the triplet state, resulting in RTP. Such structural transformation can be further used as a new doping strategy to prepare a series of water-awakened RTP phosphor@MOF composites with RTP color ranging from green to orange-red and durations of 3–6 s. Remarkably, the RTP of the composites can maintain at least six weeks when being soaked in water. Water-induced information encryption based on the composites has been demonstrated. This work not only sheds new light on the developing of water-friendly MOF-based RTP materials, but also provides a new strategy for the preparation of doped afterglow materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stimuli-responsive
Metal-organic framework
Composite
Water-induced
Room temperature phosphorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The adjustment of luminescence colors of the composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure of MOF-5 viewed along the c direction.

Prompt (a) and delayed (b) spectra of P1 (DMF solution), MOF-5, P1@MOF-5, P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O and ZnBDC⋅XH2O under 254 nm excitation under ambient conditions. (c) Time-resolved phosphorescent decay curve for the 508 nm emission peak of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O. (d) The corresponding luminescence photographs under irradiation and upon removal of a 254 nm UV lamp.

In situ time-dependent characterization of P1@MOF-5 after contact with H2O. (a) PXRD patterns. (b) The prompt spectra. (c) The delayed spectra. The curve marked “0 s” was measured immediately after the composite sample was sprayed with water.

The prompt (a) and delayed (b) PL spectra of the aqueous suspension of P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O after being stored for different times. The prompt (c) and delayed (d) PL spectra of the aqueous suspension of ZnBDC⋅XH2O after being stored for different times.

A comparison of the prompt (a) and delayed (b) luminescence spectra of a series of P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O composites with different Zn2+: P11 ratios in the starting materials (λex = 254 nm). (c) The corresponding CIE coordinates.
The prompt (a) and delayed (b) luminescence spectra of P11@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O under different excitations. (c) The corresponding CIE coordinates.

Regulation of the luminescence of the composites by changing the doped phosphors. A comparison of the prompt (a) and delayed (b) luminescence spectra of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O, P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O. (c) The corresponding CIE coordinates. (d) The corresponding luminescence photographs of the composites under irradiation and upon removal of a 254 nm UV lamp.

RTP-based anti-counterfeiting application triggered by water spray. (a) Photographs of three security painting on a nonluminescent background made from P1@MOF-5, P11@MOF-5 and P13@MOF-5 under sunlight, before and after the removal of 254 nm UV irradiation. (b) Photographs of the painting after water spray.
The organic phosphors used in this report.
Possible energy transfer pathway between doped phosphor and matrix in the composites.

head.ce:title: Water friendly room temperature phosphorescence doped materials prepared via metal organic framework matrix transformation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhang Dayu College of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimuli-responsive room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have wide applications. However, it is still a challenge to achieve water-induced recovery afterglow materials, and materials can maintain afterglow after prolonged contact with water. Herein we show that a RTP-silent metal-organic framework (MOF), MOF-5, can immediately emit green RTP upon water stimulation. Detailed studies show water causes transformation of MOF-5 into another more tightly packed framework. Besides, strong hydrogen bond network is constructed by H2O molecules trapped in the channels. Both factors limit the motion of the ligand and stabilize the triplet state, resulting in RTP. Such structural transformation can be further used as a new doping strategy to prepare a series of water-awakened RTP phosphor@MOF composites with RTP color ranging from green to orange-red and durations of 3–6 s. Remarkably, the RTP of the composites can maintain at least six weeks when being soaked in water. Water-induced information encryption based on the composites has been demonstrated. This work not only sheds new light on the developing of water-friendly MOF-based RTP materials, but also provides a new strategy for the preparation of doped afterglow materials.

Image 1
Present here is a new strategy to prepare phosphor/matrix composites with tunable room temperature phosphorescence properties evoked by water through structural transformation of the MOF matrix.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, stimuli-responsive luminescent (SRL) materials have received significant attention due to their interesting luminescence changes in response to external physical/chemical stimuli, which makes them have broad application prospects in the fields of sensing [1,2], data storage [2-6], information encryption/anti-counterfeiting [2,5,7], etc. However, so far the vast majority of the reported materials are based on singlet-state fluorescence, with lifetimes in the nanosecond scale [8-11]. In contrast, SRL materials with long-lived room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) resulting from triplet to ground state transitions, especially those exhibit recovery afterglow observable with the naked eye, are rather rare [3,6]. The long lifetime of afterglow can make them immune to short-lived background fluorescence, so it is necessary to develop afterglow-type SRL materials. In the practical application of afterglow-type SRL materials, the influence of water is an important consideration. On the one hand, among all chemical stimuli, water acts as a quenching stimulus for afterglow in the great majority of stimulus/response processes, possibly due to solvent-assisted relaxation/structural damage and dissolved oxygen [12-15]. Comparatively, examples of water as an afterglow-inducing stimulus are limited [16,17]. On the other hand, the afterglow properties of most materials show water instability after contact with water [12,18,19]. Therefore, water-induced afterglow-type SRL materials and afterglow materials whose afterglow properties can be stably maintained in water for a long time are highly desirable.

On the other respect, metal-organic framework (MOF) type RTP materials have attracted wide interest over the past few years [20-27]. The materials can be conveniently obtained through the rational combination of various inorganic and organic molecular building blocks, and have unique advantages compared with other types of RTP materials. Both the rigid structure and the coordination bond can significantly enhance the rigidity of the ligand and restrict its rotation/vibration, thereby effectively reducing the non-radiative decay of the chromophore and promoting the afterglow generation [27-31]. The heavy atom effect of metal ion is also beneficial to the afterglow emission [32-34]. Furthermore, the design concept of crystal engineering plays a key role in the preparation of such materials. Abundant stacking structures can be designed through rational stacking of molecular building blocks, thus providing various possibilities for the regulation of afterglow. So far, some MOF-based RTP materials have been reported. However, with very limited exceptions [35], water is extremely destructive to the RTP properties of the vast majority of such materials. Therefore, water-stable MOF-type RTP materials, are still being actively sought.

Doping is one of the easiest and most efficient ways to obtain RTP emission. Many materials, including inorganic salt [36-39], organics [33,40-42], polymers [43-46], carbon-dot [47-51] and inorganic-organic hybrid [20,24,52-55] materials, etc., can be used as matrices to prepare efficient RTP materials by isolating and rigidifying trace-incorporated phosphors. There are generally two strategies to achieve doping, one is in situ synthesis and the other is post-synthesis loading [23,24]. However, these two strategies are more or less dependent on the size matching of guest phosphors and host molecules. Until now, trace doping of guest molecules with much larger than that of the host has remained a great challenge. Considering the advantages of doping in the preparation of RTP materials, there is strong demand to develop new doping strategy to prepare RTP composites. So far, MOF-type RTP materials are predominantly pure compounds [56-58], and less attention has been paid to doped composites [23,24]. In view of the excellent porous and luminescent properties of MOFs, it is of great significance to conduct in-depth research on the RTP performance of MOF-based composites.

In this article, we report a new doping strategy to prepare RTP composites that can be achieved by water-stimulated matrix structural transformation. A series of initial RTP-silent phosphor@MOF-5 composites can be converted to RTP phosphor@ZnBDC⋅XH2O after water spray treatment. Thorough studies shows that the introduction of water can induce not only the conversion of MOF-5 into a more tightly packed ZnBDC⋅XH2O, but also the formation of hydrogen bond networks inside the channels. Thus the matrix can be further rigidified and the phosphor motion can be efficiently restricted, enabling the new composites to generate RTP. Phosphor@ZnBDC⋅XH2O have better quantum efficiency than that of pure ZnBDC⋅XH2O. Furthermore, their RTP exhibits amazing water stability. RTP can still be observed after the samples were immersed in water for at least six weeks. RTP color and lifetime of the composites can also be adjusted in a wide range by changing the excitation wavelength, the type or concentration of doped phosphors. Details of the structural transformation, RTP properties and application in information encryption are discussed.

1,8-naphthalimide derivatives were synthesized according to the synthesis method reported in the literature [59]. All other reagents and solvents, except additional explanation, were used as commercially purchased of analytical grade without further purification. The IR spectra were recorded (400-4000 cm−1) from a Nicolet-20DXB spectrometer using KBr pellets. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGAs) were carried out on a TA-Q50 thermogravimetric analyzer under N2 atmosphere with the heating rate of 10 °C/min. Elemental analyses (C, H, and N) were performed on a V ario EL III elemental analyzer. Powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) data were collected on D/MAX-2400 X-ray Diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54060 Å) at the scan rate of 5°/min. NMR spectra was recorded at ambient temperature on a Bruker A vance II 500 M spectrometer. The UV–visible absorption spectra were performed on a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer. The prompt, delayed emission spectra were collected on Hitachi F-7000 FL Spectrophotometer. The steady state were measured on a FLS 920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments) equipped with a microsecond flash-lamp (uF900). The photoluminescence quantum yield at room temperature was estimated using an integrating sphere accessory in FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer. Photoluminescence graphs were taken using OPPO R9 Plus under UV lamp irradiation.
MOF-5 was synthesized according to the method described in the literature [60].

The phases were prepared by following a similar procedure as that for MOF-5 with the addition of organic molecules in a molar ratio of 1/100 (phosphors/Zn2+). The obtained colorless block crystals were washed with DMF and dried in vacuum (Yield: 80% based on Zn2+).

Similar synthetic procedure but with additional addition of a series of trace amount of phosphors (P) listed in Scheme 1 leads to the formation of a series of P@MOF-5 composites. Here P1@MOF-5 is used as an example to illustrate the characterization of the composites. The PXRD pattern, IR and TGA curves of P1@MOF-5 (Fig. S1) are almost identical to those of MOF-5, but its UV absorption spectrum has one more peak (∼350 nm) with similar position to that of P1. The above experiments demonstrate that the phosphor is loaded in trace amount in the channels of MOF-5. The content of the phosphor in the composite can be determined by the luminescence standard curve method (Fig. S2). Unfortunately, the delayed spectrum of P1@MOF-5 exhibit negligible phosphorescence emission. As shown in Fig. 2a, the fluorescence peak of P1@MOF-5 is located at 412 nm, which almost coincides with that of MOF-5. For comparison, prompt spectra of P1 were measured. In DMF solution it has a fluorescence peak centered at 401 nm. However, no delayed emission peak can be observed in its solid and solution samples.

Interestingly, the composite shows some afterglow on prolonged exposure to moist air. Further experiments found that spraying water mist on the sample can induce an immediate afterglow response. The water-treated sample (named as P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O) has a yellow-green afterglow that persists for 6 s after the UV lamp excitation is switched off (Fig. 2d). Then the photoluminescence properties of the sample were measured. Upon 254 nm UV irradiation, the new phase has a fluorescence emission peaked at ∼439 nm with lifetime of 4.59 ns. The peak has a ∼27 nm red shift compared to that of P1@MOF-5. In addition, there are several shoulder peaks in the 500–550 nm region. In the delayed spectrum, a broad emission band in the 508–540 nm region with a lifetime of about 756.63 ms (508 nm, Fig. 2c) is observed.

All other P@MOF-5 composites have luminescence properties similar to that of P1@MOF-5 (Figs. S3–S23), that is, only short-lived fluorescence emission can be observed before water treatment, while afterglow begins to appear after water treatment.

In order to reveal the reasons for the above-mentioned water-induced RTP arousal in the composites, firstly in situ PXRD characterization was employed to investigate whether there was structural change during the process. The sample was first loaded into the grooves of the sample holder evenly and tightly, and then sprayed with water. Next, the time-dependent XRD patterns were collected. The result shows that new peaks at 2θ = 8.9, 15.8 and 17.8° appear in the PXRD pattern of P1@MOF-5 after contacting with water for a few minutes (Fig. 3 a), indicating the initiation of a new phase. As time goes by, the diffraction peak intensity of MOF-5 gradually weaken, while that of the new phase gradually increase. After 10 h, the diffraction peaks of MOF-5 disappear completely, and the diffraction pattern of the new phase does not change over time. A literature search reveals that the new pattern matches well with that of a reported MOF ZnBDC⋅XH2O [60]. Actually, several literature studies shown that MOF-5 undergoes structural transformation upon contact with water, generating ZnBDC⋅XH2O [60-63]. Our control experiments also demonstrate that MOF-5 alone can indeed be converted to ZnBDC⋅XH2O after being sprayed with water. Therefore, the new composite generated by the water treatment of P1@MOF-5 can be named as P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O.

In situ time-dependent emission spectra of P1@MOF-5 after contact with water were also measured. Immediately after contact with water, the fluorescence peak intensity of the sample increases rapidly, accompanied by an obvious red shift and the appearance of multiple weak emission peaks in the 500–550 nm region (Fig. 3b). As time goes by, the emission peak gradually shifts to red, its intensity shows an overall trend of increase, and both approach fixed values after about 10 h. Similarly, water treatment also results in the generation of emission peaks immediately that can be observed by the naked eye in the 508–540 nm region of the delayed spectra (Fig. 3c). The phosphorescence peaks do not change in position but increase in intensity over time, stabilizing after about 10 h.

In order to determine the source of the RTP, several control experiments were carried out. First, the solid-state sample of P1 has no phosphorescent emission at room temperature. But at 77K it has a phosphorescent emission peak in the 508–540 nm region (Fig. 2b), which is similar to that of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O. Besides, pure ZnBDC⋅XH2O was synthesized by spraying MOF-5 sample with water and subjected to luminescence control experiments. The spectrum, lifetime and quantum yield of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O are different from those of ZnBDC⋅XH2O. The prompt spectrum of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O shows several obvious shoulder peaks in the region of 500–550 nm. Time-resolved phosphorescent decay measurements show the lifetime of the emission peak at 508 nm in ZnBDC⋅XH2O is only 204.91 ms (Fig. S11), while that of the peak in P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O is 756.63 ms. Compared with that of ZnBDC⋅XH2O, the quantum yield of the P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O is also improved. The quantum yield of the former is 1.29% (Fig. S24), while that of the latter is 1.87% (Fig. S25). Although the above evidences prove that P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O and ZnBDC⋅XH2O have many differences in RTP, it is still difficult to accurately determine the main contribution of RTP of the composite exactly, because their delayed emission peaks have obvious overlap in the 506–543 nm region. Therefore, P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O was selected to further explain the contribution (Fig. S23). Compared with the green afterglow of ZnBDC⋅XH2O, P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O has orange red afterglow. Its delayed spectrum is dominated by peaks in 583–620 nm region which are contributed by P13. Therefore, all the above experiments indicate a major contribution of the phosphor guest to the phosphorescence emission of the series of composites.

The rapid RTP response of the composite to water stimulation seems to be somewhat in conflict with the relatively slow matrix transformation (from MOF-5 to ZnBDC⋅XH2O). This can be explained by the literature studies [64]. The interaction of MOF-5 with water has been investigated by molecular dynamics simulations, and it was found that the MOF-5 framework began to shrink and deform when the water content was lower than ∼2% (by mass), accompanied by a sharp decrease in the specific surface area. When the water content is greater than 4%, the framework transforms into a high-density phase, ZnBDC⋅XH2O. Such simulations were proved by experiments [65]. In addition, our control experiment also reveals that higher water content can accelerate the transformation process (Fig. S26).

Therefore, the process can be proposed as follows. Due to the large-sized channels of MOF-5, the loaded phosphors cannot be effectively fixed therein. Their vibration and rotation result in a great non-radiative decay of the excited state energy, so that RTP cannot be generated. Upon contact with water, on the one hand, the original matrix shrinks and deform immediately, and the phosphors are locked in a more rigid environment. On the other hand, MOF-5 simultaneously starts to transform into ZnBDC⋅XH2O with much smaller channels. Both changes allow for better fixation of the phosphor, which effectively reduces the non-radiative consumption, resulting in an immediate RTP response from the composite.

The above experiments reveal that after the phosphorescent guests were doped into the ZnBDC⋅XH2O matrix, the RTP emissions of the composites were dominated by the guests. This interesting phenomenon motivates us to study the energy transfer relationship between host and guest molecules. Here P1 and P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O are used as an example to illustrate the relationship. First, the UV absorption spectrum of P1 was tested and compared with the emission spectrum of ZnBDC⋅XH2O. The results show that the UV absorption spectrum of the guest overlaps only insignificantly with the emission spectrum of the host, which proves the resonance energy transfer relationship between the host and the guest is not dominant. Second, a comparison of the excitation spectra of ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P1 in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) shows they overlap well in the range of 250–360 nm (Fig. S27), suggesting the presence of competition absorption of the light source energy between the host and dopant.

Third, the UV absorption spectra of P1, ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O were compared (Fig. S27). Obvious absorption edge in the range of 400–560 nm can be observed in the curve of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O, indicating that there is a strong charge transfer between the host and guest. Furthermore, the phosphorescence peak width of P1 is narrower than that of ZnBDC⋅XH2O (Fig. 2b). The latter has a phosphorescent emission band in the 400–460 nm region while the former does not. Therefore, the phosphorescence lifetimes at 440 nm of P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O composites doped with different amounts of P1 were determined and compared with that of pure ZnBDC⋅XH2O (Fig. S28). The results show that with the increase of P1 content in the composites, the phosphorescence lifetime at 440 nm of the matrix gradually decreases from 651.04 μs to 310.66 μs, indicating that there is energy transfer between the host and guest. Based on the above experimental results, as well as the triplet excited states of the phosphor and the host are close, it is reasonable to speculate that there is energy transfer between the triplet excited state of the phosphor and the host through the pathway suggested in Scheme 2 .

For comparison, the stimulus-responsive behaviors of P1@MOF-5 to other solvents, including DMF, DMSO, EtOH, MeCN, and n-hexane, were also investigated (Fig. S29). The results show that all these solvents could not make P1@MOF-5 produce RTP response, although the fluorescence peaks in the corresponding fluorescence spectra are red-shifted to different degrees. PXRD experiments reveal that MOF-5 retains its original structure after contact with these solvents. Interestingly, organic solvents even have a certain protective effect on the MOF-5 framework, as observed in the mixed solvent (DMF/H2O) soaking experiments (Fig. S30). No afterglow is observed for the sample even after immersion for 0.5 h in a DMF/H2O mixed solvent with a water content of 50%. The results of PXRD experiments reveal that no ZnBDC⋅XH2O related diffraction peaks could be observed at this time. Weak RTP emission appears after 1.5 h of immersion, which can be observed by the naked eye. However, only after 3 h of immersion, several weak diffraction peaks attributable to ZnBDC⋅XH2O begin to appear. Obviously, RTP observation is a more convenient and sensitive means to judge whether MOF-5 has gone bad.

Although the crystal structure of ZnBDC⋅XH2O has not been reported so far, the reported specific surface analysis results show that the compound is nonporous [60-63]. Our TGA experiments found that the compound lost 17.07% of its weight before 220 °C (Fig. S31a), corresponding to the loss of 3 water molecules (calcd. 18.91%). Then there is a plateau between 220 and 450 °C. PXRD experiments show that the phase obtained by heating ZnBDC⋅XH2O at 220 °C for 20 min roughly maintains the original structure (Fig. S31b). These results suggest that ZnBDC⋅XH2O should has a microporous framework, which can be used for the loading of guest water molecules.

The presence of water molecules is generally considered to be detrimental to the RTP properties of materials because the vibrations of the O–H groups of water can deplete the excited state energy. Interestingly, although the matrix channels of the P@ZnBDC⋅XH2O composites contain water, the composites still have good RTP properties. The reason may be that strong hydrogen bonds are formed between the guest water molecules, and between the guest water molecules and the carboxylate groups in the channel wall. The presence of broad absorption bands at 2800 - 3400 cm−1 and 1680 cm−1 in the IR spectrum of the composite indicates the formation of multi-hydrogen bonds (Fig. S32) [66]. These strong hydrogen bonds can significantly rigidifies the host matrix, enhance ISC, greatly reduce the depletion of the excited state energy, thereby improving the RTP intensity effectively.

To further study the effect of the guest water molecules in the matrix channels on the RTP of the composite, P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O was selected as an example and heated at 220 °C for 20 min to remove the guest water molecules in the matrix pores, then the properties of the resulted phase (P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O-220 °C) was investigated. PXRD experiments show that the initial structure is still maintained in the heated phase (Fig. S33). The luminescence experiments show that the positions of the fluorescence peaks of the heated phase have a certain shift compared with those of the initial phase, but the position of the phosphorescence peak remains unchanged (Fig. S34). In addition, the RTP intensity and duration of the heated phase are much lower than those of the initial phase, as evidenced by the comparison of their luminescence photographs. Interestingly, the initial luminescence of the composite can be largely restored after water spray of P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O-220 °C. Control experiments with pure ZnBDC⋅XH2O also shows similar results. The above results not only further demonstrate that the guest water molecules in the matrix play an important role in the RTP behaviors of the composites, but also reveal that the reversible adjustment strong/weak RTP properties of the composite can be achieved through the adsorption and desorption of guest water molecules.

Up to now there have been several reports of water-induced RTP-type SRL materials, most of which are about carbon dots [16,17]. The role of water in these stimuli-responsive processes can basically be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bond network, which helps to further solidify the matrix and confine the phosphor motion, ultimately leading to the water-awakened RTP behavior. In this paper, the introduction of water molecules not only has the effect of forming such hydrogen bond network, but more importantly, leads to an irreversible structural transformation of the matrix, from MOF-5 with channel size of ∼12 × 12 Å2 to ZnBDC⋅XH2O with much smaller channel size. This is undoubtedly more favorable for the restriction of the motion of terephthalate ligands as well as the trace doped phosphors, which is more conducive to the RTP emission of the composites.

On the other hand, traditional doping methods usually require that the host and guest molecules have a certain degree of matching in size, which make the doping of large-sized guest molecules difficult. In this paper, we firstly use MOF-5 as the matrix to prepare non-RTP P@MOF-5 composites. Undoubtedly, the large-sized channel of MOF-5 is more conducive to the loading of large-sized guest phosphors. Then, through the structural transformation of the matrix, the phosphors loaded inside the framework are tightly fixed, and finally RTP P@ZnBDC⋅XH2O composites are formed. In this way, the requirement of size match of the host and guest phosphor molecules in the traditional doping synthesis method is avoided, which is beneficial to the doping of some large-sized phosphor molecules. As shown in Scheme 1, phosphors with size ranging from ∼5.00 × 6.57 Å2 to ∼7.07 × 12.66 Å2, some of which are larger than that of H2BDC (∼4.06 × 6.87 Å2), can be trapped in the ZnBDC⋅XH2O matrix to form RTP composites, demonstrating the advantages of this new doping strategy.

Following the above results, the effect of temperature stimuli on the luminescence of P13@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O was further investigated in the range of 70–110 °C. The luminescence spectra of the composite after heated at different temperatures were measured. The results show that the emission peaks in the fluorescence spectra show different degrees of response to the heating stimulus (Fig. S35a). The intensity ratio (I560/I439) of the two emission peaks at 560 nm and 439 nm gradually decreases with increasing temperature. This interesting relationship between the ratio of luminescence intensity and temperature can be used for ratiometric measurement of temperature. As shown in Fig. S35b, the I560/I439 ratio exhibits a good linear relationship with temperature in the range of 70–110 °C, and can be fitted to a linear function of I560/I439 = 1.457–0.00726T, indicating that the composite has potential application in temperature sensing.

The aforementioned water-awakened RTP properties of the P@MOF-5 composites motivate us to further explore whether the P@ZnBDC⋅XH2O composites still retain the RTP properties after being immersed in water. Therefore, the luminescence properties of the aqueous suspension of P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O after being stored for different times were tested and shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S36.

The results show that the composite still has naked-eye-observable RTP emission after being suspended in water. Remarkably, the RTP intensity of the suspension maintains half of its initial intensity after nine days of storage at room temperature, and a quarter of its initial intensity after 6 weeks of storage. The lifetime of the RTP peak at 583 nm after 6 weeks of storage is still 11.37 ms compared to 24.52 ms for the unsoaked sample. Both results indicate the excellent RTP water stability of the composite. Control experiments with ZnBDC⋅XH2O were also carried out. After soaking in water for 6 weeks, the sample still has good crystallinity and a lifetime of 55.78 ms at 506 nm (Fig. S37), indicating that the matrix plays a key role in maintaining the excellent water-stable RTP performance.

Thus far, although various types of RTP materials have been reported, most of their RTP can be quenched in water [12,18,19]. Some carbon dots can still maintain RTP behavior in water, the detailed structure of this class of materials is difficult to determine, which limits their further development to some degree. MOF-based RTP materials are characterized by periodic crystalline structures, which can not only provides a rigid environment to inhibit the non-radiative excited state energy loss, thereby promoting RTP generation, but also facilitates tuning the RTP performance of products through the rational assembly of inorganic and organic modules. However, a major problem of such compounds is the water destructiveness on their RTP. So far, MOF compounds that can maintain stable RTP emission in water for long periods of time are extremely rare [25,35]. Yan et al. reported a Zn-MOF compound that maintains RTP emission after being soaked in water for 10 days [35]. The composites reported in this paper have much better water stability, and their RTP emission can be maintained after immersion in water for at least 6 weeks. Furthermore, to our knowledge, this work is also the first successful study on the water stability of RTP for MOF-based composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The adjustment of luminescence colors of the composites
ce:section.ce:para: By controlling the loading amounts of phosphors, the PL color of the composites can be tuned. In the following, P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O is chosen as an example to illustrate the regulation. The RTP composite can still be prepared even with a Zn2+: P11 ratio as low as 1500 : 0.05 in the starting materials. The prompt spectrum of the resulted composite has both a short-lived peak at 442 nm and long-lived peaks in 530–645 nm region that can be attributed to the doped P11 guest molecule. With the increase of P11 content in the composites, the intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (I557/I442) first increases and reaches a maximum when the concentration ratio of Zn2+: P11 is 1500:7 (Fig. 5 a). The corresponding lifetimes at 442 nm and 557 nm are 20.7 ns and 264 ms, respectively. Then the I557/I442 ratio decreases with the increase of the concentration ratio. Correspondingly, the PL color of the composites can be regulated from blue to white, as shown in Fig. 5c. Among them, the one obtained by controlling the ratio of P11: Zn2+ in the starting material to be 1500:7, displays a (0.33,0.31) CIE coordinate, which is very close to that (0.33, 0.33) of the pure white-light-emitting (WLE). Its quantum yield is 1.37%. These data suggest that P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O has the potential to be used in WLE materials.

Interestingly, the composites also possess excitation wavelength-dependent luminescence properties. Here the composite P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O obtained by controlling the Zn2+: P11 ratio in the starting material to 1500: 7 is employed as an example to illustrate the properties. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence and phosphorescence peaks of the composite respond differently to excitation wavelengths. In this way, under the excitation of different excitation wavelengths, the relative intensity ratio of the fluorescence and the phosphorescence peaks will change, thereby realizing the adjustment of the emission color. For example, under an excitation wavelength of 254 nm, the peak intensity ratio of I557/I442 is 1.14, and the steady-state emission of the composite shows white color. As the excitation wavelength increases, the I557/I442 value gradually decrease. When the excitation wavelength is 355 nm, the value of I557/I442 decreases to 0.24, and the composite exhibits blue emission. Therefore, P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O can realize the emission color tuning from white to blue under the excitation of different wavelengths (Fig. 6c). The RTP peak position of the composite does not change under the excitation of different wavelengths and shows overall orange-red afterglow, but the intensity changes significantly. Its RTP emission reaches its maximum under 285 nm excitation.

Similarly, the emission color of the composites can also be regulated by changing the doped phosphors. The composites P1@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O, P11@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P13@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O can be prepared by controlling the ratio of Zn2+: P in the raw materials to 1500: 7. As shown in Fig. 7 a–c, under excitation at 254 nm, they exhibit blue, white and yellow luminescence colors, respectively. In addition, the RTP emission colors and afterglow lifetimes of the composites are also different. Fig. 7d shows that P1@ZnBDC⋅XH2O has a green afterglow up to ∼6 s observable to the naked eye after the 254 nm UV lamp is turned off. In comparison, P11@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P13@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O have afterglows as long as ∼2 s (yellow) and ∼1 s (orange-red), respectively. The above results indicate that these host-guest composites have the advantages of controllable preparation, flexible modulation of luminescence color and afterglow lifetime.

Since the presence of heavy groups in phosphor molecules can strongly affect RTP emission [32-34], halogen (Cl and Br) substituted P9, P10, P12 and P13 were selected as guest molecules to prepare a series of composites, and the effect of halogen groups on the luminescence of the composites was studied. Here, P12@ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O with similar structures are taken as examples to illustrate the results. As shown in Fig. S38, P12@ZnBDC⋅XH2O with Cl-substituent and P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O with Br-substituent exhibit yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp, but orange-red afterglow when the lamp is turned off, which is obviously different from the orange afterglow of P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O without halogen substituent. Further luminescence characterizations show that the RTP emission peaks of P12@ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P13@ZnBDC⋅XH2O are red-shifted by about 23 and 26 nm, and located at 580 nm and 583 nm (Figs. S22–S23), respectively, compared with the 557 nm peak of P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O. In addition, the quantum yields of P11@ZnBDC⋅XH2O, P12@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O and P13@ ZnBDC⋅XH2O are 1.37%, 6.52%, 5.51%, respectively. In contrast, the corresponding lifetimes are 271.10, 88.25, and 24.52 ms respectively.

Apparently, the halogen group in the phosphor molecule can increase the spin-orbit coupling through the internal heavy atom effect, facilitate the intersystem crossing process, and thus improve the RTP performance of the composites [32-34]. Therefore, the RTP color, lifetime and luminescence efficiency of the composites can be further regulated by introducing halogen group into phosphors.

Considering the tunable fluorescence emission color of P@MOF-5 composites, the abundant RTP color and different RTP lifetimes of P@ZnBDC⋅XH2O due to the convenient water-induced switching, P@MOF-5 composites are used to paint different patterns to verify the feasibility of application in the field of data encryption and decryption. As shown in Fig. 8 , P1@MOF-5 and P11@MOF-5 were used to paint “leaf” and “flower” patterns, respectively, while P1@MOF-5 and P13@MOF-5 were used simultaneously to paint “pedicel” and “fruit” parts of “tomato” pattern respectively. Under sunlight, all patterns are nearly colorless. But under the excitation of a 254 nm UV lamp, the patterns are activated, and vivid pictures are presented, in which the “leaf” is blue-purple and the “flower” is dark blue. For the “tomato” pattern, two luminescent colors are observed, the “pedicel” and “fruit” are blue-purple and yellow respectively. Immediately after turning off the UV lamp, all patterns turn black because P@MOF-5 composites have no afterglow. The patterns are then sprayed with water, and do not change their colors under sunlight. But under the excitation of 254 nm UV light, the color of the “leaf” pattern turns blue, the color of the " flower " pattern turns light blue and the “tomato” pattern do not change.

Impressively, all three patterns show mesmerizing afterglow when the UV lamp is turned off. The “leaf” pattern immediately turns green and can be observed by the naked eye within 6 s. The “flower” pattern turns yellow and disappears after 3 s. For the “tomato” pattern, green and orange-red afterglow are observed for its “pedicel” and “fruit” parts respectively. In addition, the afterglow of the two parts can last for 6 s and 4 s, respectively. Apparently, compared with the reported RTP-based anti-counterfeiting materials [2,5,7], the P@MOF-5 composites reported in this paper have a unique water-induced recovery RTP response, which makes them potential for high-end anti-counterfeiting applications.

In summary, two series of composites, P@MOF-5 and P@ZnBDC⋅XH2O, were synthesized. Upon water stimulation, the non-RTP former can be converted into the RTP latter. The reason of RTP arousal can be mainly attributed to the water-induced matrix structural transition from MOF-5 to ZnBDC⋅XH2O. In addition, the newly formed hydrogen bond network also has some contributions. Both of them can immobilize the host and doped molecules, suppress the nonradiative transition of excitons, and finally make the composites exhibit RTP recovery responses to water. Such structural transformation allows for the development of a new synthesis methodology for the preparation of RTP composites doped with large-sized phosphor molecules. P@ZnBDC⋅XH2O composites have extremely rare water stability, and still exhibit RTP emission after being immersed in water for at least six weeks. Furthermore, by changing the excitation wavelength, type or concentration of doped phosphors, the RTP color and lifetime of the composites can be tuned over a wide range. Finally, the P@MOF-5 series composites show attractive application prospects in the field of water-induced RTP anti-counterfeiting. This work further demonstrates the attractive development prospects of MOF compounds in the field of RTP materials, and opens up a new avenue for designing doped RTP materials.

Ai-Yun Ni: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Visualization. Bo-Lun Zhang: Investigation. Pei-Pei Zhang: Investigation. Jian-Jun Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition. Hui-Yu Wang: Investigation. Ke-Xin Feng: Investigation. Shuqin Liu: Validation. Jun Ni: Validation. Chunying Duan: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This material is based upon work supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21871038, 21471024).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110959.
